name: AI System Design Template
summary: A structured template for designing AI-powered systems, modelled on the system design interview format used at top tech companies. Use it to think through requirements, architecture, data flow, and failure modes before writing any code.
whenToUseIt: Use this template when designing a new AI system, preparing for a system design interview, or writing a technical design document for your team. It forces you to think through the critical decisions before committing to an implementation.
steps: Requirements Gathering — What is the problem? Who are the users? What are the functional requirements (what it must do) and non-functional requirements (latency, throughput, availability, cost)?
Scale Estimation — How many users? How many requests per second? How much data? What is the acceptable latency? Back-of-the-envelope calculations ground your design in reality.
High-Level Architecture — Draw the major components: data pipeline, model serving layer, API gateway, storage, monitoring. Show how data flows from input to output.
Data Pipeline Design — How does training data get collected, cleaned, and stored? How are features computed? Is it batch or streaming? How often is the model retrained?
Model Architecture — What type of model? Pre-trained foundation model, fine-tuned model, or custom trained? What is the inference pattern: real-time API, batch processing, or edge deployment?
Serving & Scaling — How is the model served? How does it scale under load? What is the caching strategy? How do you handle cold starts and traffic spikes?
Monitoring & Iteration — How do you know the system is working? What metrics are tracked? How do you detect data drift? What is the rollback plan if a new model performs worse?
example: Designing a real-time internal research assistant for a software team. Requirements: return cited answers in under 2 seconds, handle 10,000 daily queries, and support document freshness within 15 minutes of source changes. Architecture: request -> API gateway -> retrieval service -> vector index and metadata store -> model router -> response composer with citations. Monitoring tracks latency, retrieval hit rate, grounding quality, and cost per request.
